issues.title: afrind review
issues.author: afrind
issues.body:
Section 1

> Subscribers can send SUBSCRIBE messages to receive messages that arrive at

Subscribers can send SUBSCRIBE messages to receive objects that arrive at

> Such messages

These objects

> cannot omit Objects that exist at the original pulbsher

They can, using the Unknown encodings in FETCH.  Also typo “publisher”

> entirely

entirety

Section 3

> A value of zero indicates it would like to receive the entire current group; 

You should mention that the subscription filter has an argument and that’s the “value” you are talking about.

> If the subscriber wants the Groups even if SUBSCRIBE delivery semantics are not available, it MAY also send a Joining FETCH message.

Does it get both in that case?  I will read on.  Ah, I found it:

> Groups serviced via the Rewind filter are not delivered by any Joining FETCH associated with this SUBSCRIBE

Maybe add a “described below” to the first bit.

> REWIND_GROUPS is an integer that indicates the number of Groups before the LargestObject parameter that will be served via SUBSCRIBE

Mention that it is capped at the value in the rewind filter.

> any Joining FETCH associated with this SUBSCRIBE,

We now allow more than one joining FETCH per subscribe.  I think you can make this clear by referencing the “Joining Location” - e.g. Issuing a rewind advances the joining location to …
> the EndLocation reported in FETCH_OK is the highest Group ID outside the Rewind range.

Is it {Group ID, 0} exclusive?

Section 4

> If the SUBSCRIBE message includes the FORWARD parameter with value 1, the publisher MUST NOT send the REWIND_GROUPS parameter in SUBSCRIBE_OK.

Huh?  Do you mean Forward=0?

> The DELIVERY_TIMEOUT parameters for the SUBSCRIBE indicate the Group can still be sent.

Might be good to spell this out in more detail, like the object was cached less than DELIVERY_TIMEOUT ms ago.  Also mention it can’t have exceeded MAX_CACHE_DURATION?

> The publisher is not required to verify that it has all objects in the Group to include it in Rewind Groups range. In particular, Groups delivered via SUBSCRIBE might be missing objects and are still eligible for Rewind.

I think we need to be a little more strict here.  At least you need to observe the rule that you have “subgroup integrity” - e.g. if the cache has object 0 and 2, both in subgroup 0, it can’t serve them both in one stream unless it knows there is not also object 1 in that subgroup. [Ah, I see you have text, however, it might be better to reference the rules we already have].

But I also think the implicit assumption “the subscriber is getting what they would have gotten if subscribed earlier” is incomplete.  There could be some objects in cache, not others, because all previous subscribers disappeared mid group, or subscribed with different filters, etc.  I would think you need some notion of completeness at the relay to confidently do this, but perhaps subscribers don’t really care.

>  It MAY simply omit the stream with higher object IDs.

That seems not great.  This is even less “getting what they would have gotten if subscribed earlier”.


> The publisher MUST NOT send Rewind Groups > 0 if it knows it is servicing a track with Group IDs that are not strictly increasing.

How is a relay supposed to know that?

> for (object : cache.group[group_id]) {

Well a linear scan of objects in each group seems suboptimal.  One of my sadnesses with this proposal is that I really need to organize my cache both by object ID (for FETCH) and by subgroup ID (for REWIND).

I’m having a little trouble grokking the psuedocode but I can come back to it.  In general, your code is simple because you aren’t handling the gap detection - you allow rewinding to a group even if there’s only a single object there, as long as it’s the first in a subgroup.  It’s not always obvious when an object is the first in a subgroup.  You can tell when populated via FETCH, but via SUBSCRIBE when there’s more than one subgroup per group, there are challenging scenarios - any subgroup that was in the “current” group of an upstream subscribe may not start at the beginning, even if you did rewind upstream.

comments.author: martinduke
comments.body:

There could be some objects in cache, not others, because all previous subscribers disappeared mid group, or subscribed with different filters, etc. I would think you need some notion of completeness at the relay to confidently do this, but perhaps subscribers don’t really care.

The implicit assumption is something we discussed verbally, but is not in the text. IMO the paragraph about not being able to account for object IDs in a subgroup is clear and restrictive. How would you reword this?

What alternative is there? Again, this principle you're articulating is not in the draft?

>

>

How does it know not to serve FETCH in this case? I think we should have a track property.

> 
> > for (object : cache.group[group_id]) {
> 
> Well a linear scan of objects in each group seems suboptimal. One of my sadnesses with this proposal is that I really need to organize my cache both by object ID (for FETCH) and by subgroup ID (for REWIND).
>

In general, your code is simple because you aren’t handling the gap detection - you allow rewinding to a group even if there’s only a single object there, as long as it’s the first in a subgroup. It’s not always obvious when an object is the first in a subgroup. You can tell when populated via FETCH, but via SUBSCRIBE when there’s more than one subgroup per group, there are challenging scenarios - any subgroup that was in the “current” group of an upstream subscribe may not start at the beginning, even if you did rewind upstream.

OK, this is control plane code but I can add data plane

issues.title: MAX_REWIND is not actually useful
issues.author: martinduke
issues.body:
Because this is structured as an extension, there has to be a Setup Option. Because there's an option, I figured it might as well be an integer instead of a boolean.

Unfortunately, this integer isn't very useful.

In the value is zero, that's actually useful. If Group IDs aren't sequential, or the value depends on the footprint of each Group, a single session-wide value doesn't tell you much. It's just another error condition and test case.

Change it back to a boolean. If the subscriber requests a silly value, it's going to get truncated anyway.

issues.title: Failing to NextGroupStart breaks joining FETCH
issues.author: martinduke
issues.body:
If the subscriber only wants whole groups, it makes sense to fail over to NextGroupStart. But that breaks a backup Joining FETCH; there is no way to retrieve objects between LargestObject and the next group if Rewind is rejected.

We either have to revert to LargestObject (and the unneeded streams can be reset) or do something hacky with FETCH.

issues.title: how does this work with PUBLISH?
issues.author: martinduke
issues.body:
Option 1: this filter type cannot be in PUBLISH_OK, since there's no way for the publisher to respond.

Option 2: the publisher can offered REWIND_GROUPS in PUBLISH, and the subscriber can shrink it in PUBLISH_OK.

issues.title: Client complexity when combining Rewind SUBSCRIBE with Joining FETCH
issues.author: thexeos
issues.body:
Reconciling a Rewind SUBSCRIBE with a concurrent Joining FETCH adds significant state machine complexity.

The client has to speculatively issue a FETCH without knowing how much of its range will be made redundant by the rewind, then react to the REWIND_GROUPS parameter in SUBSCRIBE_OK by adjusting its internal bookkeeping of which groups it expects from which delivery path.

Some guidance or a recommended client-side approach (e.g., waiting for SUBSCRIBE_OK before issuing the FETCH, or preferred ordering) would help avoid subtle bugs here.

issues.title: Potential race between FETCH data delivery and SUBSCRIBE_OK revealing the rewind range
issues.author: thexeos
issues.body:
The draft requires the subscriber to close the session if the FETCH and rewind ranges overlap. However, from the client side, FETCH data can start arriving before SUBSCRIBE_OK is received, meaning the client may already be processing FETCH objects that later turn out to fall within the rewind range.

At that point, the client's only option is a session teardown, which feels harsh for what is essentially a timing issue rather than a protocol violation by either side. 

Is there a recommended way to sequence these messages to avoid this, or should the overlap check be relaxed to handle this window gracefully?

issues.title: No signal to the subscriber about which objects are missing in a rewound group
issues.author: thexeos
issues.body:
Draft explicitly allows publishers to include groups in the rewind range even when some objects are missing. From the subscriber's perspective, this means a rewound group can arrive with gaps and no indication of whether those gaps are "object was never produced," "object was evicted from cache," or "object is still in transit." This makes it harder for the client to make correct rendering or decoding decisions, especially for video codecs where missing dependencies affect whether later objects (frames) are usable.

This is distinct from but related to #8, which addresses the failure path where Rewind is rejected and the NextGroupStart fallback creates unreachable gaps. This issue concerns the success path: Rewind is accepted, but the delivered groups may be silently incomplete. Together they point to a broader theme of the client lacking sufficient information about incomplete data during the join flow.

Would it be worth recommending that publishers signal incomplete groups somehow, or at least documenting what clients should assume when objects are absent from a rewound group?

issues.title: Extend rewind to support relative and absolute fill
issues.author: wilaw
issues.body:
OTT players need a reliable mechanism for joining a live stream. Typically they want 1-3 groups behind live.  The rewind draft currently indicates

> If the subscriber wants the Groups even if SUBSCRIBE delivery semantics are not available, it MAY also send a Joining FETCH message, as described below.

Since the client cannot know ahead of a time if the Groups they need to start are available in the cache, sending a Joining FETCH then is a de facto MUST for a client which needs reliability.

Instead of having the client have to two send messages to join, can we extend Rewind to implement the behavior described in https://afrind.github.io/draft-frindell-moq-join-filters/draft-frindell-moq-join-filters.html. 

That document defines two new MOQT Subscription Filters -- Join Relative Group and Join Absolute Group -- that enable a subscriber to receive both historical and live content on a single subscription. Unlike Joining FETCH, which delivers past objects on a single stream with head-of-line blocking, Join filters deliver historical content using SUBSCRIBE semantics: per-subgroup streams, subject to delivery timeout, priorities, and datagram forwarding preferences. The publisher uses a combination of cache and upstream FETCH to assemble complete groups for delivery.

Join Relative Group is identical to Rewind filter in syntax. 

To modify Rewind proposal to support the features of both Join Relative Group and Join Absolute , we would only need to add a byte flag with 3 states
0 - no fill, just give me what you have in cache
1 - use the Rewind Groups attribute as an absolute Group ID
2 - use the Rewind Groups attribute as a relative offset

This would give a clean and consistent interface for clients joining the stream and avoid the need for the client to have to make a parallel joining FETCH. In fact, this would allow us to remove Joining FETCH as a message.

comments.body:
It can block some objects in cache, that might eventually be deliverable, but weren't before the gaps were filled.

e.g.

cache has sg=0, objects: 0, 10; gaps: 1-9, 11-max

With a FETCH it determines that objects 1-9 are in on other subgroups or don't exist --> it can send 0,10 in the same stream.
right, they are not deliverable at the moment the fetch is issued
